title: SEGMENT 18 — Frontier — platforms, in vivo editing, EU and Chinese pathways, AI-driven trial design

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — Frontier — platforms, in vivo editing, EU and Chinese pathways, AI-driven trial design
Let me close with the frontier.
Where is gene therapy regulation going?
What are the developments that will shape the next decade of how these drugs get developed, reviewed, and approved?
I want to touch on five things.
They are partially independent and partially interrelated, and they are all reshaping the field as we speak.
First, platform technologies.
I just mentioned the platform technology designation in the context of milasen.

The idea is that instead of treating each ASO sequence, each CRISPR guide, each CAR construct as a separate drug requiring a separate IND, you treat the platform — the chemistry, the delivery mechanism, the manufacturing process — as the regulated entity, and individual sequences become smaller variations within an established framework.
The FDA has been developing thinking on this through a 2024 designation called the Platform Technology Designation, codified in the FDORA legislation.
The expectation is that this designation will reduce the regulatory burden for N-of-1 and ultra-rare disease therapeutics by an order of magnitude, perhaps more.
Whether it lives up to that promise depends on how the agency implements it in practice over the next several years, but the direction is clear.
Second, in vivo CRISPR and in vivo base editing.
The first wave of CRISPR therapeutics — Casgevy, Lyfgenia, others in development — are ex vivo.
You take cells out of the patient, edit them in the lab, put them back in.
The next wave is in vivo.
You inject the editing machinery directly into the patient and let the editing happen inside their body.

This is technically much harder, but the payoff is bigger — no need for stem cell collection, no need for conditioning chemotherapy, much broader applicability to diseases where you cannot collect and engraft the target cell type.
Verve Therapeutics and others have shown promising early results with in vivo base editing of PCSK9 to lower cholesterol.
Intellia has shown promising in vivo CRISPR editing of TTR for transthyretin amyloidosis.
These programs are in the clinic now.

The regulatory framework for in vivo editing — what kind of biodistribution data does the agency want, what kind of off-target characterization, what kind of long-term follow-up — is being defined as they progress.
The next decade of gene therapy regulation will be substantially shaped by these in vivo programs.
Third, the EU CAT and PRIME pathway.
I have mentioned the European framework in passing throughout this lecture.
The EMA's Committee for Advanced Therapies — CAT — and the broader EU regulatory framework for advanced therapy medicinal products have been quietly evolving.

The PRIME designation, the adaptive licensing approaches, the hospital exemption that allows individual hospitals to manufacture customized ATMPs for individual patients without full marketing authorization — all of these are EU-specific mechanisms that, in some cases, are more flexible than the corresponding US pathways.

For sponsors developing globally, the strategy increasingly involves engaging with both FDA and EMA in parallel, leveraging the different flexibilities of each system, and timing approvals to maximize market entry.
Fourth, the Chinese NMPA pathway.
The Chinese National Medical Products Administration — NMPA — has been rapidly building its capacity for cell and gene therapy review.

China has substantial domestic biotech investment in this space, a regulatory framework that has been modernizing fast, and a patient population that is in some categories larger than any other single jurisdiction.
NMPA approvals of cell and gene therapies are accumulating, and the regulatory standards are converging — slowly, with continuing differences — toward those of FDA and EMA.
For a global gene therapy program, the question is no longer "FDA or EMA first.
" It is "FDA, EMA, and NMPA in what order, with what local trial requirements, with what local manufacturing footprint.
" The three-jurisdiction strategy is the modern reality.
Fifth, AI-driven trial design.
The use of artificial intelligence and machine learning in clinical trial design, patient stratification, and regulatory analytics is exploding.
Agencies are exploring how to incorporate AI-derived evidence into their decision-making.
Sponsors are using AI to design more efficient trials, to identify which patients are most likely to respond, to predict adverse events, to mine real-world evidence at scale.
The regulatory framework for AI in drug development is, frankly, still being written.
The FDA has issued draft guidance, but the field is moving faster than the guidance can.

Over the next decade, expect to see AI play a substantially larger role in how gene therapy trials are designed and reviewed, and expect the agency to develop new frameworks for evaluating AI-derived evidence.
So that is the frontier.
Platform designations that compress the regulatory burden for ultra-rare therapeutics.
In vivo editing that expands the addressable disease space.
EU and Chinese pathways that complement the US framework.
AI that reshapes trial design and analytical evidence.
Each of these is reshaping the field.
Each of them is creating new regulatory questions that the agencies — FDA, EMA, CAT, NMPA — are scrambling to address.
If I close with a single observation, it is this.
The regulatory system for gene therapy is, today, more flexible, more sophisticated, and more open to scientific innovation than it has ever been in the history of medicine.
The agencies have built mechanisms for accelerated development, for accommodating small patient populations, for handling unique mutations, for supporting platform technologies.
The mechanisms are imperfect.
The system is still slow and expensive.
But it is not the rigid, formulaic obstacle that the popular caricature sometimes makes it out to be.
It is, in the best moments, a partner in figuring out how to bring new therapies to patients responsibly.
That partnership has produced Luxturna, Zolgensma, Hemgenix, Casgevy, Lyfgenia, and milasen — and the partnership will produce, over the next decade, hundreds more.
The science gets the credit when a new gene therapy is approved.
The regulatory system mostly stays invisible.
But the invisibility is, in some sense, the system working as intended.
You only notice the regulator when something is wrong.

When the system works — when a sponsor and an agency together find a path from a molecular insight to a patient receiving a transformative one-time treatment — the work of the regulator disappears into the background, and the science takes the stage.
That is, I think, the right framing.
Not the regulator as obstacle.
The regulator as co-architect.
The gauntlet as a feature, not a bug.
The system that produces the discipline that produces the medicines that change lives.
Thank you for staying with me through two hours of regulatory detail.
The path from concept to approved gene therapy is long, expensive, uncertain, and gated at every step.
The path also works.
Patients are alive today because of gene therapies that, twenty years ago, did not exist.
The framework we have walked through today is what made that possible.
I will take questions for the next fifteen minutes.
Then I will be in my office for the rest of the afternoon if you want to come by and discuss any specific program or any specific regulatory mechanism in more detail.
Good luck on your problem sets.
